lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Nancy Drew? Oh, thank heavens. I wasn't sure anyone would come.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Ms. Delacroix? You called about a poisoning — and a ghost?

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Both, dear. Roland Ashworth — the man demolishing my theater — collapsed last night. Doctors say he was poisoned. And before that... she appeared again.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: She?

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Margaux Fontaine. The greatest actress who ever stood on that stage. She died here in 1928 — on stage, during her final performance. And now her ghost walks the Monarch.

choices.text: Tell me about Margaux Fontaine.
What happened to Ashworth?
Who else is in the theater right now?

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Margaux Fontaine was a legend. She was performing 'The Crimson Veil' — her signature role — when she collapsed during the final act. Poison in the prop goblet, they said. An accident.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: But I never believed that. She was my godmother, Nancy. She held me as a baby. And I've spent my whole life in this theater, waiting for the truth.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You think she was murdered?

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: I know she was. And I think her ghost knows it too.

choices.text: What happened to Ashworth?
I'll start investigating.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Roland Ashworth bought the Monarch six months ago. Plans to demolish it in three days and build luxury condominiums.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Last night, he was found unconscious in his office. Some kind of toxin — non-lethal, but deliberate. He's recovering upstairs, furious and suspicious of everyone.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Does he have enemies here?

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Everyone in this theater has a reason to want the demolition stopped. Myself included. I won't pretend otherwise.

choices.text: Tell me about Margaux Fontaine.
I'll start looking around.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: There's Edwin Hale — a theater historian. He's been documenting the Monarch for years. You'll find him in the auditorium, practically living in those seats.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Stella Morrow is our stage manager. Practical woman, knows every inch of this building. She's backstage.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: And young Diego Reyes — a playwright renting the projection booth. Sweet boy, but he's heard things at night he can't explain.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Then there's Ashworth himself, recovering in the manager's office. He won't be happy to see a detective.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: I left a master key at the concierge desk. It opens most doors in the theater. There's also a magnifying glass — my late husband's.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: And Nancy — be careful. The Monarch has kept its secrets for almost a century. It won't give them up easily.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Vivian, I found something in the dressing room. Margaux's diary.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Oh... oh my. I always hoped it survived. What does it say?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: She suspected she was being poisoned. Long before the final night. 'The tea tastes wrong again,' she wrote.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: I knew it. I always knew it wasn't an accident.

choices.text: She mentions someone with the initials 'C.D.' — does that mean anything to you?
Tell me about the trunk in the dressing room.
Did Margaux have a lover? The diary mentions someone named 'J.'

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: C.D... Cecilia Drake. Margaux's understudy.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: She was young, ambitious, talented. Margaux adored her — took her under her wing. But Cecilia wanted more than mentorship. She wanted the role.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: And after Margaux died—

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Cecilia took over Lady Ashford the very next night. She became a star. Built a forty-year career on the role she stole.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: She died in 1987. Celebrated. Beloved. While Margaux was forgotten.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Margaux's trunk! I remember it from when I was small. She kept her most precious things inside.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: The combination was always a date — Margaux used dates she could never forget. She called it 'my last Halloween.' The night everything changed.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: October 31st...

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: 1928. The night of the final performance.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: J? I... yes. Margaux had a great love. A man named James — James Hale. He was a stagehand here at the Monarch.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Hale? As in Edwin Hale?

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Edwin is James's grandson. That's why he's so obsessed with this theater, with Margaux's story. It's personal for him. His grandfather lost the love of his life on that stage.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: That changes everything.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Nancy... promise me something. Whatever you find out — whoever is responsible, past or present — make sure Margaux's name is cleared. She deserves that much.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Vivian, I found this in Margaux's trunk.

choices.text: Show her the locket.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: That's... that's me. I was four years old.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: 'To my little star.' She called me that. Every time she held me.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: She kept it in the trunk. All these years. She locked it away because she knew... she knew she wouldn't be coming back.

choices.text: She wanted to protect what mattered most.
Vivian — do you know who killed her?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: She wanted to protect what mattered most.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: I've been waiting seventy years to know what happened to her. I've lived my whole life in this theater because I couldn't leave her behind.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: There are private archives in my office — Margaux's personal effects, letters, photographs. I've never let anyone touch them. Take whatever you need.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Don't let this building come down without the truth coming out. Please.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: I was four years old when she died. I remember the screaming. I remember someone carrying me out of the theater.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: I've always suspected Cecilia. She was the understudy. She took the role. She never looked back. But I could never prove it.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'm going to find the proof. I promise.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: My office — there are private archives. Margaux's personal effects. Take whatever you need. Just bring me the truth.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Ah — you must be the detective Vivian called. Edwin Hale. Theater historian. Please, sit anywhere — the whole auditorium's empty, as usual.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Nancy Drew. How long have you been studying the Monarch?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Fifteen years. I've written three books on New England theater history. The Monarch is my life's work.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: This building isn't just bricks and velvet, Ms. Drew. It's a living archive. Every floorboard has a story. And if Ashworth has his way, it'll all be rubble in seventy-two hours.

choices.text: Tell me about The Crimson Veil.
What do you think about the ghost sightings?
Did you see anything suspicious last night?
Your grandfather was James Hale — Margaux's lover.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: The Crimson Veil was the jewel of the 1928 season. A gothic melodrama about a countess who discovers her husband has been poisoning her. Ironic, isn't it?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Margaux Fontaine was extraordinary in the role. In the final act, her character drinks from a poisoned goblet. But on opening night — October 31st — the prop goblet contained real poison.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: And it was ruled an accident?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: The official story was that a stagehand made a terrible mistake. But the stagehand was never identified. And Margaux's understudy, Cecilia Drake, took over the role the very next night and became a star.

choices.text: You think Cecilia Drake killed her?
I found Margaux's diary. She knew she was being poisoned.
Thank you, Edwin. I'll keep investigating.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: You found her diary? Where? In the dressing room?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: ...May I see it? Please. I've been looking for primary sources about Margaux for fifteen years.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: She writes about feeling sick after tea, about a metallic taste. And she suspected someone specific.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Then she knew. She knew, and nobody listened. The police, the theater owners — they all called it an accident because it was easier.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: That diary could be the evidence we need. Don't lose it, Nancy.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I'm a historian, not a spiritualist. But I've seen things here I can't explain.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Fog that rolls across the stage when no machine is running. A figure in white that appears under the ghost light and vanishes. The sound of a woman's voice reciting lines from The Crimson Veil.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Whether it's truly Margaux's spirit or something else entirely... this theater is not at peace.

choices.text: The effects manual describes systems that could fake all of that.
Tell me about The Crimson Veil.
Interesting. I'll keep investigating.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: You've read the effects manual? Impressive — most people wouldn't bother with a century-old tech document.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Yes, theoretically someone could use the old systems to stage a convincing ghost. Fog machines, the trapdoor, proper lighting...

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: But they'd need extensive knowledge of the building. They'd need to have practiced. And they'd need a reason.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Like saving the theater from demolition?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: ...That would certainly be a reason. Hypothetically.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I was in my usual seat — row G, center — reviewing my notes. Around ten o'clock, I heard a crash from the manager's office. By the time I got there, Ashworth was on the floor.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Stella called the ambulance. The rest of us waited. It was... unsettling.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Did you notice anything else? Anyone missing?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Everyone was accounted for. But this is a big building with many hidden corners. Someone could have slipped away without being seen.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I have my suspicions. Cecilia Drake had means, motive, and opportunity. She knew the prop table. She had access backstage. And she had everything to gain.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: But proving it after nearly a century? That's the challenge. If there's evidence, it's buried somewhere in this theater.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Then I'd better start digging.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: ...Who told you that?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Vivian. She recognized the name.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Yes. James Hale was my grandfather. He was a stagehand here in the twenties. He and Margaux...

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: He never got over her death. He spent the rest of his life insisting it wasn't an accident. Nobody believed him. He died in 1971, still fighting.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I promised him I'd find the truth. That's why I'm here. That's why I've always been here.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: That's a heavy promise to carry for fifteen years.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Some promises are worth the weight.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: If you need anything — history, floor plans, old records — I'm your man. I know this building better than anyone alive.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Oh, and Nancy? If you see the ghost... try talking to her. You never know.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Detective? Great. Another person poking around my backstage. I'm Stella Morrow — stage manager. Or I was, until Ashworth decided this place was worth more as rubble.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You don't seem happy about the investigation.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I'm not happy about any of this. A man was poisoned, there's supposedly a ghost, and in three days this whole building comes down. I just want to do my job and go home.

choices.text: Has anything gone missing from backstage?
Do you know how the special effects work?
Where were you when Ashworth collapsed?
I found your note to the basement intruder — 'Stop now or I go to the police.'

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Some things have... been relocated. For safekeeping. Valuable antique props, costumes — they shouldn't be destroyed with the building.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Relocated where, exactly?

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Look, those props are irreplaceable. I did what I had to do. That doesn't make me a poisoner.

choices.text: I found your lockbox. $4,200 in sold props — is that for your mother's medical bills?
I'm not accusing you. Tell me about the effects system.
We'll come back to the props later.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: You opened my—

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Yes. My mother has stage four lymphoma. The bills are... I can't keep up. Those props were going to be destroyed in three days anyway. At least this way someone gets something.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'm not here about the props, Stella. I'm here about the poisoning.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I know who's been in the basement. I've known for weeks. I wrote him a note. He ignored it.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Who?

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Edwin. It's always been Edwin.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I've seen him going down there at night. I know the sounds — the trapdoor mechanism, the fog machine warming up. He's been staging the ghost.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I didn't tell anyone because... honestly? I wanted the theater saved too. If a ghost story kept the demolition from happening, I wasn't going to stop it.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: But then Ashworth got poisoned, and I realized Edwin had gone too far. I left him that note. He never responded.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Where's the basement key?

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Behind the lighting panel. Edwin thinks he's the only one who knows, but I've been stage manager here for eight years. I know every hiding spot in this building.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Where did you find that?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: On the stage floor. 'Stop now or I go to the police — S.M.' You wrote it.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I wrote it three days ago. Slipped it under the basement door. Whoever's down there never responded.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You know who's in the basement.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I have a pretty good idea. But I need to know — are you going to report what I've done? The props?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'm interested in the truth, Stella. All of it.

choices.text: Tell me who's in the basement and I'll focus on the poisoning.
Let's talk about the effects system first.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: The Monarch was state-of-the-art in 1925. Fog machines, trapdoors, a fly system, even hidden passages behind the walls for quick costume changes.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: There should be an effects manual on the tech shelf — a big binder. It has diagrams of everything.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Could someone use those systems to fake a ghost?

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: If they knew what they were doing? Absolutely. Fog, a trapdoor entrance, the right lighting cue... you could make someone appear and vanish in seconds.

choices.text: Who would know the systems well enough?
I've already read the manual. Someone's added fresh notes to it.
Thanks, Stella. I'll check out that manual.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Fresh notes? Let me see...

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: This handwriting — it's meticulous, almost academic. I've seen writing like this on the call board. On notes left around the theater.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: This looks like Edwin Hale's handwriting.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Me, obviously. Edwin Hale probably — he's studied every inch of this place. Vivian grew up here, so she'd know the basics.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: But the hidden passages? Those aren't common knowledge. You'd need the original blueprints or the effects manual to even know they exist.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I was right here, backstage, doing inventory. When I heard the crash I ran to the office and called 911. You can check the call log.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I didn't poison anyone. I don't even drink tea.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: One more thing — there's a locked door at the back of backstage that leads to the basement. I don't have the key. Nobody's supposed to. But I've heard that door opening and closing at night.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Whatever's going on in this theater... it's coming from down there.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Stella, I found blueprints with hidden passages marked. Do you know about these?

choices.text: Show her the blueprints.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I knew about the passages — the effects manual mentions them. But I've never seen a complete map like this.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Look — this passage connects the basement to the dressing room. And this one goes from behind the orchestra pit to the lobby. Someone could move through the entire building without ever being seen in the open.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Someone like our ghost.

choices.text: Have you ever been in the passages yourself?
Can you get me to the catwalk?

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Once. Last month I heard footsteps behind the backstage wall at 2 AM. I told myself it was the building settling.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: But buildings don't settle in rhythm. Someone was walking. And they knew exactly where they were going.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Someone who's studied these blueprints.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: There's only one person who knows this theater well enough to navigate those passages in the dark. And he's been here every day for fifteen years.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: You need to get to the catwalk. I'll unlock the ladder for you. There's a lighting board up there — if someone's been running a light show, that's where they set it up.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Exactly what I was thinking. I'll unlock the ladder for you. There's a lighting board up there that controls the whole stage. If someone's been running a light show, that's where they set it up.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Thank you, Stella. You've been more helpful than you know.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Just... be careful up there. The catwalk is original 1920s construction. And whatever you find — remember that not everyone in this theater is a villain. Some of us are just trying to survive.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Oh! Sorry — didn't hear you come up. I get lost in my writing. Diego Reyes. I'm renting the projection booth as a studio.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Nancy Drew. I'm investigating the poisoning — and the ghost.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: The ghost is real. I mean — I've heard her. At night, from up here, I can hear someone reciting lines on the empty stage. Act three of The Crimson Veil. Word for word.

choices.text: Have you found anything unusual up here?
What have you heard at night?
I decoded the script annotations — it spells 'goblet.'

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Actually, yes. I found an original script of The Crimson Veil in one of the film canisters. Handwritten annotations all through the margins in red ink.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: I thought it was blocking notes at first, but the marks are weird. Circled letters that don't match any stage directions I know. It's like a code.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: How many circled letters are there?

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: I counted six in Act III alone. G, O, B, L, E, T — but I couldn't figure out what they mean. They're scattered through the stage directions, not the dialogue.

choices.text: Can I take a closer look at it?

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Be my guest. It's right here on my desk. I've been trying to crack it for weeks.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: You know what's weird? The annotations are only in Act III — the act where Lady Ashford drinks from the goblet. Whoever wrote these notes was focused on that specific scene.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Goblet? As in — the prop goblet from the play?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: The circled letters in order: G-O-B-L-E-T. Someone annotated the script to record that the goblet was the murder weapon.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Holy... that means someone knew. Someone wrote it down in code in the margins of the actual script. And that script has been sitting in a film canister up here for almost a century.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: The question is who wrote the annotations. And when.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: The ink is old — really old. That's not modern handwriting. Whoever coded that message did it close to 1928.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: It started about a month ago. I stay late — the booth is quiet, good for writing. Around midnight, I hear footsteps below. Then fog drifts across the stage.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Last week, I heard what sounded like machinery — grinding, mechanical sounds from under the stage. Like old gears turning.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: And then one night... I looked down from the booth window and saw a woman in white, standing center stage. She turned, looked up — right at me — and vanished. Through the floor.

choices.text: Through the floor? Like a trapdoor?
The effects manual describes a trapdoor system in the stage floor.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: That's... actually a really good point. I didn't think of it as a trapdoor because — well, ghosts don't need trapdoors.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: But a person would.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Wait — you're saying the theater has an actual trapdoor in the stage? That someone could use to appear and disappear?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: The 1925 effects manual describes a counterweight system that can lift a person from the basement to center stage in seconds.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: So the ghost isn't floating through the floor. Someone is riding an elevator through it.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: I feel stupid. I'm a playwright. I should know about trapdoors.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Hey, one more thing — I've been writing a play about all this. 'The Ghost of the Monarch.' My agent says it's the best thing I've ever written.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: I hope the real ending is worth writing about too.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Another vulture. What do you want?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Nancy Drew. I'm here to find out who poisoned you, Mr. Ashworth.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Oh, take your pick. Everyone in this building wants me dead. The old actress, the historian, even my own stage manager. This theater is full of people who'd rather see me in the ground than see one brick moved.

choices.text: What happened the night you collapsed?
Someone marked up your blueprints. Was that you?
You mentioned seeing someone in the building at night.
Your wall safe contains insurance documents. The demolition is worth $2.3 million — you rejected an $800K offer to sell.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: I was working late. Had a cup of tea from the kettle in here — same as every night. Within twenty minutes, my vision blurred and I couldn't stand.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Before I blacked out, I saw... someone. A shape in the doorway. They were watching me fall.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Can you describe them?

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Dark clothes. Average build. They didn't help. They just watched, and then they were gone.

choices.text: I found the teacup. The residue looks like antimony — a metallic poison.
Can I see the blueprints on your wall?
I'll examine the office.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Antimony? That's... that's an old-fashioned poison. Victorian era. Who even knows how to use that?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Someone who's been researching a murder from 1928.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Hale. You're talking about Hale, aren't you. That man and his obsession with the dead actress.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'm not accusing anyone yet. But the connection between the compounds used on you and the ones used on Margaux Fontaine is... significant.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Get him out of my theater. Get him out and let me tear this place down in peace.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: How did you — that safe was locked.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: The Historical Society offered you $800,000 for the theater. You turned it down because the demolition insurance pays $2.3 million.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: That's... a business decision. Not a crime.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: It's a motive. If the theater gets landmark status, you can't demolish it. You lose the insurance payout and have to accept the lower offer. Everyone in this building wants to save the theater — and you have a $1.5 million reason to destroy it.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: I'm the victim here, Drew. Someone poisoned ME. Don't turn this around.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: I didn't draw those red marks. They were already there when I pinned them up. Someone's been mapping hidden passages, sealed rooms, a tunnel network beneath the stage.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: This theater is a maze. Someone could move through this building without ever being seen.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Do you know who made the annotations?

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: My money's on the historian. Hale. He's obsessed with this place.

choices.text: Tell me about the intruder you found.
Thank you, Mr. Ashworth.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Two weeks ago, I came in early and heard noises from below the stage. Mechanical sounds. I found the basement door unlocked and went down.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Someone had set up a workshop down there. Tools, documents, chemicals. I couldn't see everything — they heard me coming and the lights went out. I barely made it back upstairs.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: That's when the 'ghost' sightings got worse. And three days later, I was poisoned.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You think they poisoned you to keep you quiet?

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: I don't think. I know.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Find whoever did this. Fast. Because in seventy-two hours, this building comes down regardless.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Ghost or no ghost.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Hello, Edwin.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: ...Nancy. I was wondering when you'd find your way down here.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: The fog machines. The trapdoor. The white costume. Your notebook on the catwalk. You're the ghost of the Monarch Theatre.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: And you're as good as Vivian said you were.

choices.text: Why, Edwin? Why all of this?
You poisoned Ashworth.
Your grandfather was James Hale. This was never just about the theater.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: ...You know about my grandfather?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: James Hale. He was a stagehand here. He loved Margaux. He spent his whole life trying to prove she was murdered.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: He died when I was twelve. The last thing he said to me was: 'Promise me you'll find the truth about Margaux.' I was a kid. I didn't understand what he meant.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: But I grew up. I became a historian. And fifteen years ago, I started keeping that promise.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: And six months ago, you found the proof.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Cecilia Drake's letters. Hidden in the basement wall. Proof that she ordered the poison. Proof that she swapped the goblet. Proof that my grandfather was right all along.

choices.text: So why the ghost? Why not just go public?
But then you poisoned Ashworth. You became the very thing you were investigating.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Fifteen years I've been researching this theater. And six months ago, I found them — Cecilia Drake's letters to a pharmacist. She ordered the exact compound that killed Margaux.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I had the proof. But Ashworth had the demolition permits. In three days, this evidence would be buried under concrete. The historical society's petition was stalled in review. So I did the only thing I could think of.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I made Margaux's ghost real enough to matter. If the Monarch was haunted — truly haunted — the media attention could force a landmark designation. The demolition would be delayed. The truth would come out.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You used her costume, her blocking, her lines. You studied the effects manual and rebuilt the original systems.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I spent six months down here, rehearsing. Learning the trapdoor timing, the fog sequences, the lighting cues. I wanted it to be perfect. I wanted her to be seen one last time.

choices.text: But then Ashworth caught you.
That's beautiful. But you crossed a line.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Ashworth found the basement. He saw my setup. He was going to call the police and accelerate the demolition — tear it down the next morning if he could.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I used antimony tartrate — the same compound from Margaux's alchemy book. Non-lethal dose. Just enough to make him too ill to sign demolition orders for a few days.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You used the same poison that killed Margaux.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: ...I know. The irony isn't lost on me.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You poisoned a man, Edwin. You became exactly what you were investigating — someone who poisons people to get what they want.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I saved a theater. I saved a woman's legacy. I saved the truth about a murder that went unsolved for a century.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Was it wrong? Yes. Would I do it again?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: ...Yes.

choices.text: I'm calling the police. You poisoned someone, Edwin.
Let's go public. The 1928 murder, the cover-up — all of it. Together.
Walk away, Edwin. Disappear. I'll make sure Margaux's story gets told.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I understand. I knew this was how it might end.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Just... promise me one thing. Make sure the letters get to the right people. Margaux deserves to be remembered for more than how she died.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I promise.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: My grandfather kept his promise for fifty years. You'll keep yours. I can tell.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Sometimes the truth isn't enough to save what we love. But it's always enough to matter.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Together? You'd do that?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: The world should know what happened to Margaux Fontaine. And they should know what you did to protect her memory — all of it. The heroism and the crime.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I'll face whatever comes. But the Monarch...

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: We'll give it the best chance we can. The 1928 murder, the cover-up, the landmark petition — we put it all in front of the public and let them decide.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Then let's give the Monarch one last opening night.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: The Monarch's lights came on for the first time in years. Not for a ghost — for the woman who deserved to be remembered.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: You'd let me go?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'm letting Margaux's story be told. What happens to you... that's between you and your conscience.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: ...Thank you, Nancy. Tell Vivian I'm sorry I couldn't say goodbye.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Edwin.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Yes?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Your grandfather would be proud of what you found. Not how you found it. But what you found.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I know. That's the part I'll have to live with.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I told myself it was the right call. I still tell myself that. Most days, I believe it.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: An old rotary phone on the lobby wall. It still has a dial tone — the theater's landline hasn't been disconnected yet.

choices.text: Call Bess and George.
Call Dad.
Look up the local Historical Society.
Call Ned.
Try the Hardy boys' number.
Hang up.

lines.speaker: Bess
lines.text: Nancy! How's the haunted theater? George and I looked it up — the Monarch Theatre was designed by the same architect who built the Ryerson Opera House. He loved hidden passages.

lines.speaker: George
lines.text: I ran some searches. Cecilia Drake had a long career after 1928. She won three theater awards and never once mentioned Margaux Fontaine in any interview. Not once in forty years.

lines.speaker: Bess
lines.text: That's suspicious, right? If your mentor died tragically and you took over her role, you'd at least mention her?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Definitely. Oh — the stage tech here, Diego, has been really helpful. He knows the theater inside and out.

lines.speaker: Bess
lines.text: Diego? Is he cute?

lines.speaker: George
lines.text: Bess!

lines.speaker: Bess
lines.text: What? It's a valid investigative question!

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'll call back when I know more. About the case, Bess. The case.

lines.speaker: Carson Drew
lines.text: Nancy! How's the case?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Complicated, Dad. There's a modern poisoning connected to a possible murder from 1928. And the theater is being demolished in three days.

lines.speaker: Carson Drew
lines.text: The statute of limitations on a 1928 murder expired decades ago — you can't prosecute Cecilia Drake even if she did it. But if the evidence proves murder, it could qualify the theater for landmark protection under the Cultural Heritage Act.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: So proving Margaux was murdered could save the theater?

lines.speaker: Carson Drew
lines.text: If the evidence is strong enough, yes. A site of a documented historical crime qualifies for emergency heritage review. That would halt the demolition.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Thanks, Dad. That changes everything.

lines.speaker: Receptionist
lines.text: River Heights Historical Society, how may I help you?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'm calling about the Monarch Theatre landmark petition.

lines.speaker: Receptionist
lines.text: Ah yes — Mr. Hale's petition. It's been in review for three months. The problem is, we need evidence of significant historical events at the site. The architecture alone isn't enough.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: What if I could prove that a murder took place there in 1928?

lines.speaker: Receptionist
lines.text: That would certainly qualify. If you can provide documented evidence, we can file for an emergency heritage review. That would legally halt any demolition until the review is complete.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I may have exactly what you need. I'll be in touch.

lines.speaker: Ned
lines.text: Nancy! I was hoping you'd call. How's the haunted theater?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Complicated. There's a murder from 1928, a modern poisoning, a fake ghost, and the building might be demolished in three days.

lines.speaker: Ned
lines.text: So... a normal Tuesday for you. Just promise me you'll be careful? Old buildings have a way of falling apart at the worst possible moment.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I always am. Well, mostly.

lines.speaker: Ned
lines.text: That's what worries me. Call me when you solve it — I know you will.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I dial a number I've had memorized for years. It rings twice, then clicks to voicemail.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: 'You've reached Frank and Joe Hardy. We're probably on a case. Leave a message and we'll call you back — unless we're tied up. Literally. Again.' *beep*

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Hey, it's Nancy. I'm at the Monarch Theatre in River Heights — haunted theater, 1928 murder, the works. If you're ever in town, I could use a second opinion. Or a third. Talk soon.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I set the receiver back in its cradle. The phone clicks and goes silent.
